description: Exact same single-turn chat request

content.text: What is the capital of France?

response.content: Paris is the capital of France.

content.text: What is the capital of France?

expected.note: Identical request must always hit the O(1) direct path.

description: Same request with different capitalisation — normalised to the same hash

content.text: what is the boiling point of water?

response.content: Water boils at 100°C (212°F) at sea level.

content.text: WHAT IS THE BOILING POINT OF WATER?

expected.note: normalizeText() lowercases before hashing, so case variants collide in the same bucket.

description: Exact same multi-turn conversation

content.text: Who invented the printing press?
Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press around 1440.
Where was he from?

response.content: Gutenberg was from Mainz, in the Holy Roman Empire (present-day Germany).

content.text: Who invented the printing press?
Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press around 1440.
Where was he from?

expected.note: Identical multi-turn conversation hashes to the same UUIDv5.

description: Same request with cache_type forced to direct-only

content.text: What year did World War II end?

response.content: World War II ended in 1945.

content.text: What year did World War II end?

expected.note: cache_type=direct skips semantic path; identical request still hits direct.

description: Paraphrase: capital of France

content.text: What is the capital of France?

response.content: Paris is the capital of France.

content.text: Which city serves as the capital of France?

expected.note: Synonym rephrasing; cosine similarity should comfortably exceed 0.85.

description: Paraphrase: machine learning explanation

content.text: Explain machine learning in simple terms

response.content: Machine learning is a branch of AI where systems learn from data to make predictions without being explicitly programmed.

content.text: What is machine learning, explained simply?

expected.note: Same intent, reordered phrase; strong semantic overlap.

description: Paraphrase: why does it rain

content.text: What causes rain?

response.content: Rain forms when water vapour in clouds cools, condenses into droplets, and falls due to gravity.

content.text: Why does it rain?

expected.note: 'What causes X' and 'Why does X happen' are semantically equivalent.

description: Paraphrase: telephone inventor

content.text: Who invented the telephone?

response.content: Alexander Graham Bell is credited with inventing the telephone in 1876.

content.text: Who is credited with inventing the telephone?

expected.note: Active vs passive phrasing of the same factual question.

description: Paraphrase: speed of light

content.text: What is the speed of light?

response.content: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second.

content.text: How fast does light travel?

expected.note: 'What is the speed of X' vs 'How fast does X travel' — near-identical meaning.

description: Paraphrase: French Revolution summary

content.text: Summarize the French Revolution briefly

response.content: The French Revolution (1789–1799) was a period of radical political and societal change in France that overthrew the monarchy and established a republic.

content.text: Give me a brief overview of the French Revolution

expected.note: 'Summarize briefly' vs 'give a brief overview' — interchangeable intent.

description: Similar structure, different country: France vs Germany

content.text: What is the capital of France?

response.content: Paris is the capital of France.

content.text: What is the capital of Germany?

expected.note: Structurally identical template with a different named entity — must not cross-contaminate.

description: Related phenomenon but different: rain vs snow

content.text: What causes rain?

response.content: Rain forms when water vapour in clouds cools, condenses into droplets, and falls due to gravity.

content.text: What causes snow?

expected.note: Rain and snow are related precipitation types but have different answers — a hit here is a harmful false positive.

description: Adjacent ML concepts: machine learning vs deep learning

content.text: Explain machine learning in simple terms

response.content: Machine learning is a branch of AI where systems learn from data to make predictions without being explicitly programmed.

content.text: Explain deep learning in simple terms

expected.note: Deep learning is a subset of ML. Answering 'deep learning' with an 'ML' response is technically wrong.

description: Similar invention template: telephone vs telegraph

content.text: Who invented the telephone?

response.content: Alexander Graham Bell is credited with inventing the telephone in 1876.

content.text: Who invented the telegraph?

expected.note: Telephone (Bell) vs telegraph (Morse) — different inventors, dangerous false positive.

description: Similar physics question: speed of light vs speed of sound

content.text: What is the speed of light?

response.content: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second.

content.text: What is the speed of sound?

expected.note: Both are 'speed of X' physics questions with very different numeric answers.

description: Photosynthesis conversation, same topic paraphrased

content.text: What is photosynthesis?
Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce glucose and oxygen.
What plants use it most?

response.content: Tropical rainforest plants, with their large leaf surfaces and year-round sunlight, are among the most active photosynthesizers.

content.text: Can you explain photosynthesis?
Photosynthesis is a biological process where plants convert light energy into chemical energy, producing food and releasing oxygen.
Which plants use it the most?

expected.note: Same topic, same follow-up intent — full conversation embedding should be highly similar.

description: Napoleon conversation, paraphrased phrasing

content.text: Who was Napoleon Bonaparte?
Napoleon Bonaparte was a French military leader and emperor who rose to prominence during the French Revolution.
When did he die?

response.content: Napoleon Bonaparte died on May 5, 1821, on the island of Saint Helena.

content.text: Tell me about Napoleon
Napoleon was a famous French military commander and statesman who became Emperor of France.
What year did he pass away?

expected.note: Same person, same follow-up question in different phrasing — full conversation embedding aligns.

description: Ambiguous follow-up: rivers 'second largest' vs monuments 'second largest'

content.text: What is the largest river in Europe?
The Volga River, stretching 3,530 km, is the largest river in Europe.
What is the second largest?

response.content: The Danube is the second largest river in Europe at approximately 2,860 km.

content.text: What is the largest monument in Europe?
The Eiffel Tower is considered one of the most iconic monuments in Europe.
What is the second largest?

expected.note: Core risk case: shared last message 'what is the second largest?' must NOT pull a river answer into a monument query.

description: Ambiguous follow-up: fastest animal vs fastest vehicle

content.text: What is the fastest animal on Earth?
The cheetah is the fastest land animal, reaching speeds up to 120 km/h.
What is the second fastest?

response.content: The pronghorn antelope is the second fastest land animal, reaching speeds of about 88 km/h.

content.text: What is the fastest production vehicle?
The Bugatti Chiron Super Sport holds a top speed record of approximately 490 km/h.
What is the second fastest?

expected.note: Animals vs vehicles: different domains despite identical last user message.

description: Ambiguous follow-up: tallest mountain vs tallest building

content.text: What is the tallest mountain in Asia?
Mount Everest, at 8,849 metres, is the tallest mountain in Asia and the world.
What is the second tallest?

response.content: K2 is the second tallest mountain at 8,611 metres.

content.text: What is the tallest building in Asia?
The Merdeka 118 in Kuala Lumpur, at 678.9 metres, is the tallest building in Asia.
What is the second tallest?

expected.note: Mountains vs buildings: same ordinal question pattern, entirely different subject matter.

description: Same prompt, different model — must not cross model boundaries

content.text: What is the capital of Japan?

response.content: Tokyo is the capital of Japan.

content.text: What is the capital of Japan?

expected.note: Model name participates in directCacheID (UUIDv5) and the semantic filter — different model must miss.

description: Same prompt, different provider — must not cross provider boundaries

content.text: What is the capital of Italy?

response.content: Rome is the capital of Italy.

content.text: What is the capital of Italy?

expected.note: Provider name participates in directCacheID and the semantic filter — different provider must miss.

description: Same prompt, different temperature — params_hash differs, semantic filter rejects

content.text: What is the capital of Spain?

response.content: Madrid is the capital of Spain.

content.text: What is the capital of Spain?

expected.note: Temperature is hashed into params_hash; the semantic search filter rejects entries with a different params_hash.

description: 4-message conversation exceeds ConversationHistoryThreshold=3 — Lookup returns (nil, nil, nil)

content.text: Tell me about the Roman Empire
The Roman Empire was one of the most powerful civilisations in history.
When did it fall?
The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD.

response.content: 476 AD

content.text: Tell me about the Roman Empire
The Roman Empire was one of the most powerful civilisations in history.
When did it fall?
The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD.

expected.note: 4 messages > threshold (3): Lookup returns (nil, nil, nil). No entry is written or read.

description: 3-message conversation exactly at threshold — proceeds normally and hits direct cache

content.text: What is the largest ocean?
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean, covering about 165 million square kilometres.
What is the second largest?

response.content: The Atlantic Ocean is the second largest, covering about 106 million square kilometres.

content.text: What is the largest ocean?
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean, covering about 165 million square kilometres.
What is the second largest?

expected.note: 3 messages == threshold: 3 > 3 is false, so caching proceeds. Identical request hits the direct path.

description: Same request with leading/trailing whitespace — trimmed by normalizeText before hashing

content.text: What is the largest ocean?

response.content: The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.

content.text: What is the largest ocean?

expected.note: normalizeText trims spaces before hashing — whitespace variants land in the same bucket.

description: Text-type (non-chat) exact prompt match

request.prompt: Summarize the water cycle in two sentences.

response.content: Water evaporates from surfaces, rises and condenses into clouds, then falls as precipitation and returns to bodies of water. This continuous movement is driven by solar energy and gravity.

request.prompt: Summarize the water cycle in two sentences.

expected.note: Text-type requests are hashed via the prompt field; identical prompt hits the direct path.

description: Paraphrase: how to reverse a string in Python

content.text: How do I reverse a string in Python?

response.content: You can reverse a string in Python using slicing: reversed_str = my_str[::-1]

content.text: What is the Pythonic way to reverse a string?

expected.note: Same programming task, different phrasing — embedding similarity should be high.

description: Paraphrase: Newton's first law

content.text: What is Newton's first law of motion?

response.content: Newton's first law states that an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion, unless acted on by an external force.

content.text: Can you explain Newton's first law of motion?

expected.note: 'What is X' vs 'Can you explain X' — high semantic overlap on the same named concept.

description: Paraphrase: distance from Earth to the Moon

content.text: How far is the Moon from Earth?

response.content: The average distance from the Earth to the Moon is about 384,400 kilometres.

content.text: What is the distance between the Earth and the Moon?

expected.note: 'How far is X from Y' vs 'What is the distance between X and Y' — semantically identical.

description: Paraphrase: author of Pride and Prejudice

content.text: Who wrote Pride and Prejudice?

response.content: Pride and Prejudice was written by Jane Austen, published in 1813.

content.text: Who is the author of Pride and Prejudice?

expected.note: 'Who wrote X' vs 'Who is the author of X' — interchangeable intent.

description: Paraphrase: symptoms of the flu

content.text: What are the symptoms of the flu?

response.content: Common flu symptoms include fever, chills, muscle aches, cough, congestion, headache, and fatigue.

content.text: What symptoms does the flu cause?

expected.note: 'What are the symptoms of X' vs 'What symptoms does X cause' — same medical inquiry.

description: Paraphrase: sorting a list in Python

content.text: How do I sort a list in Python?

response.content: Use list.sort() to sort in-place, or sorted(list) to return a new sorted list.

content.text: What is the Python method to sort a list?

expected.note: Same Python task rephrased; strong topical and lexical overlap.

description: Newton's first law vs Newton's second law — same template, different concept

content.text: What is Newton's first law of motion?

response.content: Newton's first law states that an object at rest stays at rest unless acted on by an external force.

content.text: What is Newton's second law of motion?

expected.note: First law (inertia) vs second law (F=ma) — serving the wrong law is a factual error. Threshold raised to 0.95 to prevent false positive (similarity ~0.90).

description: Flu symptoms vs flu causes — same disease, different question

content.text: What are the symptoms of the flu?

response.content: Common flu symptoms include fever, chills, muscle aches, cough, congestion, headache, and fatigue.

content.text: What are the causes of the flu?

expected.note: Symptoms vs causes of the same disease — entirely different answers despite identical disease name.

description: Distance to Moon vs distance to Mars — same template, different bodies

content.text: How far is the Moon from Earth?

response.content: The average distance from the Earth to the Moon is about 384,400 kilometres.

content.text: How far is Mars from Earth?

expected.note: Moon (~384,400 km) vs Mars (~225 million km) — different celestial body, very different numeric answer.

description: Sort list in Python vs sort array in JavaScript — same task, different language

content.text: How do I sort a list in Python?

response.content: Use list.sort() to sort in-place, or sorted(list) to return a new sorted list.

content.text: How do I sort an array in JavaScript?

expected.note: Python sort syntax differs from JavaScript — returning Python code for a JavaScript question is wrong.

description: Different book authors — Pride and Prejudice vs Crime and Punishment

content.text: Who wrote Pride and Prejudice?

response.content: Pride and Prejudice was written by Jane Austen, published in 1813.

content.text: Who wrote Crime and Punishment?

expected.note: Austen vs Dostoevsky — serving the wrong author is a clear factual error.

description: Climate change conversation, same topic paraphrased

content.text: What is climate change?
Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns, primarily driven by human activities since the 1800s.
What are the main causes?

response.content: The main causes are burning fossil fuels, deforestation, and industrial processes that release greenhouse gases like CO2 and methane.

content.text: Can you explain climate change?
Climate change is the long-term alteration of temperature and typical weather patterns, largely caused by human activity such as burning fossil fuels.
What causes it?

expected.note: Same topic, same follow-up intent ('causes') — full conversation embedding should align.

description: Python list operations conversation, paraphrased

content.text: How do I create a list in Python?
You create a list in Python using square brackets: my_list = [1, 2, 3]
How do I add an element to it?

response.content: Use the append() method: my_list.append(4). This adds 4 to the end of the list.

content.text: What's the syntax for creating a list in Python?
In Python, lists are created with square brackets: items = [1, 2, 3]
How can I add items to the list?

expected.note: Same Python topic and follow-up; paraphrased phrasing of both turns.

description: Ambiguous follow-up 'how long does it take?' — cooking vs travel context

content.text: I want to make pasta from scratch
Making pasta from scratch involves mixing flour and eggs, then kneading the dough until smooth.
How long does it take?

response.content: Making fresh pasta from scratch takes about 45 to 60 minutes including resting time for the dough.

content.text: I want to travel from New York to London
There are regular direct flights from New York's JFK airport to London Heathrow operated by several airlines.
How long does it take?

expected.note: Cooking time (~1 hr) vs flight time (~7 hrs) — shared follow-up must not cross domains.

description: Ambiguous follow-up 'what are the main causes?' — WWI vs inflation context

content.text: Tell me about World War I
World War I was a global conflict from 1914 to 1918, primarily fought in Europe between the Allied and Central Powers.
What are the main causes?

response.content: The main causes were nationalism, imperialism, militarism, and the alliance system — remembered by the acronym MAIN.

content.text: Tell me about inflation
Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices for goods and services rises, reducing purchasing power over time.
What are the main causes?

expected.note: WWI causes (MAIN) vs inflation causes (demand-pull, cost-push) — completely different answers.

description: Ambiguous follow-up 'who is considered the greatest?' — scientists vs artists context

content.text: Who were the most influential scientists of the 20th century?
Among the most influential are Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, Richard Feynman, and Niels Bohr.
Who is considered the greatest?

response.content: Albert Einstein is widely considered the greatest scientist of the 20th century for his theory of relativity.

content.text: Who were the most influential artists of the 20th century?
Key figures include Pablo Picasso, Salvador Dalí, Andy Warhol, and Jackson Pollock.
Who is considered the greatest?

expected.note: Einstein (science) vs Picasso (art) — returning Einstein's name for an art question is factually wrong.

description: Same prompt, different cache_key — tenant scope must be isolated

content.text: What is the capital of Brazil?

response.content: Brasília is the capital of Brazil.

content.text: What is the capital of Brazil?

expected.note: cache_key partitions entries by tenant; tenant-b must not read tenant-a entries.

description: Same prompt, chat vs chat_stream — stream flag is in params_hash so buckets differ

content.text: What is the capital of Canada?

response.content: Ottawa is the capital of Canada.

content.text: What is the capital of Canada?

expected.note: buildParamsMetadata adds stream=true/false to the params map; different stream flag → different params_hash → separate bucket.

description: Very short greeting paraphrase: 'Hi' vs 'Hello'

content.text: Hi

response.content: Hello! How can I help you today?

content.text: Hello

expected.note: Single-word greetings with equivalent meaning; embedding models typically score these very highly.

description: Verbose vs concise phrasing of the same factual question

content.text: What is the capital of France?

response.content: Paris is the capital of France.

content.text: Could you please tell me what the capital city of France is?

expected.note: Polite padding words do not change meaning; the core semantic content is identical.

description: Different arithmetic: square root of 16 vs cube root of 27

content.text: What is the square root of 16?

response.content: The square root of 16 is 4.

content.text: What is the cube root of 27?

expected.note: Different operations and operands; both are 'root of N' questions but answering 4 for a cube-root query is wrong.

description: Different data structures: linked list vs binary tree

content.text: What is a linked list?

response.content: A linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains a value and a pointer to the next node.

content.text: What is a binary tree?

expected.note: Both are 'What is a data structure' questions but define entirely different structures.

description: British vs American English spelling — colour vs color

content.text: What is the colour of the sky?

response.content: The sky appears blue during the day due to Rayleigh scattering of sunlight.

content.text: What is the color of the sky?

expected.note: British vs American spelling of the same word; embedding models treat these as semantically identical.
